text.KJV: 23:21 But do not thou yeeld vnto them: for there lie in wait for him of them moe then fourtie men, which haue bound themselues with an othe, that they will neither eate nor drinke, till they haue killed him: and now are they ready, looking for a promise from thee.

text.Base: So the tribune dismissed the young man, ordering him, “Tell no one that you have informed me of this.”
text.KJV: 23:22 So the chiefe captaine then let the yong man depart, and charged him, See thou tell no man, that thou hast shewed these things to me.

text.Base: Then he summoned two of the centurions and said, “Get ready to leave for Caesarea by nine o’clock tonight with two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen.
text.KJV: 23:23 And he called vnto him two Centurions, saying, Make ready two hundred souldiers to goe to Cesarea, and horsemen threescore and ten, and spearemen two hundred, at the third houre of the night.

text.Base: Also provide mounts for Paul to ride, and take him safely to Felix the governor.”
text.KJV: 23:24 And prouide them beasts, that they may set Paul on, and bring him safe vnto Felix the gouernour.

text.Base: He wrote a letter to this effect: “Claudius Lysias to his Excellency the governor Felix, greetings.
text.KJV: 23:25 And hee wrote a letter after this manner:

text.Base: This man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed by them, but when I had learned that he was a Roman citizen, I came with the guard and rescued him.
text.KJV: 23:27 This man was taken of the Iewes and should haue beene killed of them: Then came I with an armie, and rescued him, hauing vnderstood that he was a Romane.

text.Base: Wishing to know the charge for which they accused him, I had him brought to their council.
text.KJV: 23:28 And when I would haue knowen the cause wherefore they accused him, I brought him foorth into their Council.

text.Base: I found that he was accused concerning questions of their law, but was charged with nothing deserving death or imprisonment.
text.KJV: 23:29 Whom I perceiued to be accused of questions of their lawe, but to haue nothing laide to his charge worthy of death or of bonds.

text.Base: When I was informed that there would be a plot against the man, I sent him to you at once, ordering his accusers also to state before you what they have against him.”
text.KJV: 23:30 And when it was tolde me, how that the Iewes laid waite for the man, I sent straightway to thee, and gaue commandement to his accusers also, to say before thee what they had against him. Farewell.

text.Base: So the soldiers, according to their instructions, took Paul and brought him during the night to Antipatris.
text.KJV: 23:31 Then the souldiers, as it was commaunded them, tooke Paul, and brought him by night to Antipatris.

text.Base: The next day they let the horsemen go on with him, while they returned to the barracks.
text.KJV: 23:32 On the morow, they left the horsemen to goe with him, and returned to the castle.

text.Base: When they came to Caesarea and delivered the letter to the governor, they presented Paul also before him.
text.KJV: 23:33 Who when they came to Cesarea, and deliuered the Epistle to the gouernour, presented Paul also before him.

text.Base: On reading the letter, he asked what province he belonged to, and when he learned that he was from Cilicia,
text.KJV: 23:34 And when the gouernour had read the letter, he asked of what prouince he was. And when he vnderstood that he was of Cilicia:

text.Base: he said, “I will give you a hearing when your accusers arrive.” Then he ordered that he be kept under guard in Herod’s headquarters.
text.KJV: 23:35 I will heare thee, said hee, when thine accusers are also come. And hee commanded him to be kept in Herods iudgement hall.

text.Base: Five days later the high priest Ananias came down with some elders and an attorney, a certain Tertullus, and they reported their case against Paul to the governor.
text.KJV: 24:1 And after fiue dayes, Ananias the hie Priest descended with the Elders, and with a certaine Oratour named Tertullus, who enformed the gouernour against Paul.

text.Base: When Paul had been summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying: “Your Excellency, because of you we have long enjoyed peace, and reforms have been made for this people because of your foresight.
text.KJV: 24:2 And when he was called foorth, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying, Seeing that by thee we enioy great quietnesse, and that very worthy deeds are done vnto this natiō by thy prouidence:

text.Base: In every way and everywhere we welcome this with utmost gratitude.
text.KJV: 24:3 Wee accept it alwayes, and in all places, most noble Felix, with all thankfulnesse.

text.Base: But, to detain you no further, I beg you to hear us briefly with your customary forbearance.
text.KJV: 24:4 Notwithstanding, that I be not farther tedious vnto thee, I pray thee, that thou wouldest heare vs of thy clemencie a few words.

text.Base: We have, in fact, found this man a pestilent fellow, an agitator among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazoreans.
text.KJV: 24:5 For we haue found this man a pestilent fellow, and a moouer of sedition among all the Iewes throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes.

text.Base: He even tried to profane the temple, and so we seized him.
text.KJV: 24:6 Who also hath gone about to profane the Temple: whom we tooke, and would haue iudged according to our lawe.

text.Base: By examining him yourself you will be able to learn from him about everything of which we accuse him.”
text.KJV: 24:8 Commanding his accusers to come vnto thee, by examining of whom thy selfe mayest take knowledge of all these things, whereof we accuse him.

text.Base: The Jews also joined in the charge by asserting that all this was true.
text.KJV: 24:9 And the Iewes also assented, saying that these things were so.

text.Base: When the governor motioned to him to speak, Paul replied: “I cheerfully make my defense, knowing that for many years you have been a judge over this nation.
text.KJV: 24:10 Then Paul, after that the gouernour had beckened vnto him to speake, answered, Forasmuch as I know that thou hast been of many yeeres a Iudge vnto this nation, I do the more cheerefully answere for my selfe:

text.Base: As you can find out, it is not more than twelve days since I went up to worship in Jerusalem.
text.KJV: 24:11 Because that thou mayest vnderstand, that there are yet but twelue dayes, since I went vp to Hierusalem for to worship.

text.Base: They did not find me disputing with anyone in the temple or stirring up a crowd either in the synagogues or throughout the city.
text.KJV: 24:12 And they neither found me in the Temple disputing with any man, neither raising vp the people, neither in the Synagogues, nor in the citie:

text.Base: Neither can they prove to you the charge that they now bring against me.
text.KJV: 24:13 Neither can they proue the things whereof they now accuse me.

text.Base: But this I admit to you, that according to the Way, which they call a sect, I worship the God of our ancestors, believing everything laid down according to the law or written in the prophets.
text.KJV: 24:14 But this I confesse vnto thee, that after the way which they call heresie, so worship I the God of my fathers, beleeuing all things which are written in the Law and the Prophets,

text.Base: I have a hope in God—a hope that they themselves also accept—that there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the unrighteous.
text.KJV: 24:15 And haue hope towards God, which they themselues also allow, that there shall be a resurrection of the dead, both of the iust and vniust.

text.Base: Therefore I do my best always to have a clear conscience before God and before people.
text.KJV: 24:16 And herein doe I exercise my selfe to haue alwayes a conscience void of offence toward God, and toward men.

text.Base: Now after some years I came to bring alms to my nation and to offer sacrifices.
text.KJV: 24:17 Now after many yeeres, I came to bring almes to my nation, & offrings:

text.Base: While I was doing this, they found me in the temple, purified, with no crowd or disturbance.
text.KJV: 24:18 Wherupon certaine Iewes from Asia found me purified in the Temple, neither with multitude, nor with tumult:

text.Base: But there were some Jews from Asia—they ought to be here before you to make an accusation, if they have anything against me.
text.KJV: 24:19 Who ought to haue beene here before thee, and obiect, if they had ought against me.

text.Base: Or let these men here tell what crime they had found when I stood before the council,
text.KJV: 24:20 Or else let these same here say, if they haue found any euill doing in mee, while I stood before the Councill,

text.Base: unless it was this one sentence that I shouted while standing among them, ‘It is about the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial before you today.’ ”
text.KJV: 24:21 Except it be for this one voice, that I cried standing among them, Touching the resurrection of the dead I am called in question by you this day.

text.Base: But Felix, who was rather well informed about the Way, adjourned the hearing with the comment, “When Lysias the tribune comes down, I will decide your case.”
text.KJV: 24:22 And when Felix heard these things, hauing more perfect knowledge of that way, he deferred them and said, When Lysias the chiefe captaine shall come downe, I will know the vttermost of your matter.

text.Base: Then he ordered the centurion to keep him in custody, but to let him have some liberty and not to prevent any of his friends from taking care of his needs.
text.KJV: 24:23 And he commanded a Centurion to keepe Paul, and to let him haue libertie, and that he should forbid none of his acquaintance to minister, or come vnto him.

text.Base: Some days later when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish, he sent for Paul and heard him speak about faith in Christ Jesus.
text.KJV: 24:24 And after certaine dayes, when Felix came with his wife Orusilla, which was a Iew, he sent for Paul, and heard him cōcerning the faith in Christ.

text.Base: And as he discussed justice, self-control, and the coming judgment, Felix became frightened and said, “Go away for the present; when I have an opportunity, I will send for you.”
text.KJV: 24:25 And as he reasoned of righteousnesse, temperance, and iudgement to come, Felix trembled and answered, Go thy way for this time, when I haue a conuenient season, I will call for thee.

text.Base: At the same time he hoped that money would be given him by Paul, and for that reason he used to send for him very often and converse with him.
text.KJV: 24:26 He hoped also that money should haue bene giuen him of Paul, that hee might loose him: wherefore hee sent for him the oftner, and cōmuned with him.

text.Base: After two years had passed, Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus; and since he wanted to grant the Jews a favor, Felix left Paul in prison.
text.KJV: 24:27 But after two yeeres, Portius Festus came into Felix roome: and Felix willing to shew the Iewes a pleasure, left Paul bound.

text.NRSV: Three days after Festus had arrived in the province, he went up from Caesarea to Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: Festus, therefore, having come upon the province, after three days, went up unto Jerusalem from Caesarea;
text.KJV: 25:1 Nowe when Festus was come into the prouince, after three dayes he ascended frō Cesarea to Hierusalem.

text.NRSV: The chief priests and the leaders of the Jews reported their case against Paul, and they appealed to him
text.Rotherham: and the High-priest and chiefs of the Jews laid information before him against Paul, and began to beseech him
text.KJV: 25:2 Then the high Priest, and the chiefe of the Iewes informed him against Paul, and besought him,

text.NRSV: and requested, as a favor to them against Paul, to have him transferred to Jerusalem, for they were planning an ambush to kill him on the way.
text.Rotherham: asking for themselves as a favour against him, that he would send for him unto Jerusalem,—making, an ambush, to kill him on the way.
text.KJV: 25:3 And desired fauour against him, that he would send for him to Hierusalem, laying wait in the way to kill him.

text.NRSV: Festus replied that Paul was being kept at Caesarea, and that he himself intended to go there shortly.
text.Rotherham: Festus, therefore, answered, that Paul should be kept in Caesarea, and that, he himself, was about, shortly, to be going out [thither] .
text.KJV: 25:4 But Festus answered, that Paul should be kept at Cesarea, and that hee himselfe would depart shortly thither.

text.NRSV: “So,” he said, “let those of you who have the authority come down with me, and if there is anything wrong about the man, let them accuse him.”
text.Rotherham: They, therefore, among you (saith he) who are in power, let them go down with me; and, if there is in the man, anything amiss, let them accuse him.
text.KJV: 25:5 Let them therefore, said he, which among you are able, go downe with me, and accuse this man, if there be any wickednesse in him.

text.NRSV: After he had stayed among them not more than eight or ten days, he went down to Caesarea; the next day he took his seat on the tribunal and ordered Paul to be brought.
text.Rotherham: And, spending among them, not more than eight or ten days, he went down unto Caesarea; and, on the morrow, taking his place upon the judgment-seat, ordered Paul to be brought.
text.KJV: 25:6 And when hee had taried among them more then ten dayes, hee went downe vnto Cesarea, and the next day sitting in the iudgement seat, commanded Paul to be brought.

text.NRSV: When he arrived, the Jews who had gone down from Jerusalem stood around him, bringing many serious charges against him, which they could not prove.
text.Rotherham: And, when he presented himself, the Jews who, from Jerusalem, had come down, stood round about him, many and grievous charges, bringing against [him], which they were not able to prove,—
text.KJV: 25:7 And when hee was come, the Iewes which came downe from Hierusalem, stood round about, and laide many and grieuous complaints against Paul, which they could not proue,

text.NRSV: Paul said in his defense, “I have in no way committed an offense against the law of the Jews, or against the temple, or against the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: Paul saying in defence—Neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar, have I in anything sinned.
text.KJV: 25:8 While hee answered for himselfe, Neither against the law of the Iewes, neither against the Temple, nor yet against Cesar, haue I offended any thing at all.

text.NRSV: But Festus, wishing to do the Jews a favor, said to Paul, “Do you wish to go up to Jerusalem and there be tried on these charges before me?”
text.Rotherham: But, Festus, wishing, with the Jews, to gain, favour, answered Paul, and said—Art thou willing, unto Jerusalem, to go up, and, there, concerning these things, be judged before me?
text.KJV: 25:9 But Festus willing to doe the Iewes a pleasure, answered Paul, and said, Wilt thou goe vp to Hierusalem, and there be iudged of these things before me?

text.NRSV: Paul said, “I am appealing to the emperor’s tribunal; this is where I should be tried. I have done no wrong to the Jews, as you very well know.
text.Rotherham: But Paul said—Standing before the judgment-seat of Caesar, am I, where, I, ought to be judged. Unto the Jews, have I done no wrong, as, even thou, right well, art discovering.
text.KJV: 25:10 Then said Paul, I stand at Cesars iudgement seat, where I ought to bee iudged; to the Iewes haue I done no wrong, as thou very well knowest.

text.NRSV: Now if I am a wrongdoer and have committed anything for which I deserve to die, I am not trying to escape death; but if there is nothing to their charges against me, no one can turn me over to them. I appeal to the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: If then, on the one hand, I am doing wrong, and, anything worthy of death, have committed, I excuse not myself from dying; but, on the other hand, if there is, nothing, in the things whereof these are accusing me, no man, hath power to give, me, unto them as a favour:—Unto Caesar, I appeal!
text.KJV: 25:11 For if I be an offender, or haue committed any thing worthy of death, I refuse not to die: but if there be none of these things whereof these accuse me, no man may deliuer me vnto them. I appeale vnto Cesar.

text.NRSV: Then Festus, after he had conferred with his council, replied, “You have appealed to the emperor; to the emperor you shall go.”
text.Rotherham: Then Festus, having conversed with the council, answered—Unto Caesar, hast thou appealed? Unto Caesar, shalt thou go.
text.KJV: 25:12 Then Festus when he had conferred with the Councill, answered, Hast thou appealed vnto Cesar? vnto Cesar shalt thou goe.

text.NRSV: After several days had passed, King Agrippa and Bernice arrived at Caesarea to welcome Festus.
text.Rotherham: And, some days having gone by, Agrippa the king and Bernice came down to Caesarea, to salute Festus.
text.KJV: 25:13 And after certaine dayes, king Agrippa and Bernice, came vnto Cesarea, to salute Festus.

text.NRSV: Since they were staying there several days, Festus laid Paul’s case before the king, saying, “There is a man here who was left in prison by Felix.
text.Rotherham: And, as they were spending more days there, Festus, repeated, unto the king, the things relating to Paul, saying—A certain man, hath been left behind by Felix, as a prisoner;
text.KJV: 25:14 And when they had beene there many dayes, Festus declared Pauls cause vnto the king, saying, There is a certaine man left in bonds by Felix:

text.NRSV: When I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews informed me about him and asked for a sentence against him.
text.Rotherham: concerning whom, when I happened to be in Jerusalem, the High-priests and the Elders of the Jews laid information, claiming against him a condemnation:
text.KJV: 25:15 About whom when I was at Hierusalem, the chiefe Priests and the Elders of the Iewes enformed me, desiring to haue iudgement against him.

text.NRSV: I replied that it was not the custom of the Romans to hand over anyone before the accused had met the accusers face to face and had been given an opportunity to make a defense against the charge.
text.Rotherham: unto whom made answer—That it is not a custom with Romans, to grant as a favour any man, before the accused, face to face, should have his accusers, and, opportunity of defence, should receive, concerning the charge.
text.KJV: 25:16 To whom I answered, It is not the maner of the Romanes to deliuer any man to die, before that he which is accused, haue the accusers face to face, and haue licence to answere for himselfe concerning the crime laid against him.

text.NRSV: So when they met here, I lost no time, but on the next day took my seat on the tribunal and ordered the man to be brought.
text.Rotherham: When, therefore, they had come together here, no delay whatever, making, on the next day, taking my place upon the judgment-seat, I ordered the man to be brought:
text.KJV: 25:17 Therefore when they were come hither, without any delay, on the morrow I sate on the iudgement seate, and cōmanded the man to be brought forth.

text.NRSV: When the accusers stood up, they did not charge him with any of the crimes that I was expecting.
text.Rotherham: Concerning whom, taking their stand, his accusers, no accusation at all, were bringing, of the evil things which, I, had been suspecting;
text.KJV: 25:18 Against whom when the accusers stood vp, they brought none accusation of such things as I supposed:

text.NRSV: Instead they had certain points of disagreement with him about their own religion and about a certain Jesus, who had died, but whom Paul asserted to be alive.
text.Rotherham: but, certain questions concerning their own demon-worship, had they against him, and concerning one Jesus, who had died, whom Paul was affirming to be alive.
text.KJV: 25:19 But had certaine questions against him of their owne superstition, and of one Iesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be aliue.

text.NRSV: Since I was at a loss how to investigate these questions, I asked whether he wished to go to Jerusalem and be tried there on these charges.
text.Rotherham: And, I, being at a loss as to the inquiry into these things, was asking—whether he might be minded to go unto Jerusalem, and, there, be judged concerning these things.
text.KJV: 25:20 And because I doubted of such maner of questions, I asked him whether he would goe to Hierusalem, and there be iudged of these matters.

text.NRSV: But when Paul had appealed to be kept in custody for the decision of his Imperial Majesty, I ordered him to be held until I could send him to the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: But, Paul, having appealed to be kept for the decision of the Emperor, I ordered him to be kept, until I could send him up unto Caesar.
text.KJV: 25:21 But when Paul had appealed to bee reserued vnto the hearing of Augustus, I commanded him to be kept, till I might send him to Cesar.

text.NRSV: Agrippa said to Festus, “I would like to hear the man myself.” “Tomorrow,” he said, “you will hear him.”
text.Rotherham: And, Agrippa, [said] unto Festus—I could wish, myself also, to hear, the man. To-morrow, (saith he) thou shalt hear him.
text.KJV: 25:22 Then Agrippa said vnto Festus, I would also heare the man my selfe. To morrow, said he, thou shalt heare him.

text.NRSV: So on the next day Agrippa and Bernice came with great pomp, and they entered the audience hall with the military tribunes and the prominent men of the city. Then Festus gave the order and Paul was brought in.
text.Rotherham: On the morrow, therefore, when Agrippa had come, and Bernice, with great display, and they had entered into the audience-chamber, with the captains of thousands and men of distinction of the city,—and Festus had given orders, Paul was brought.
text.KJV: 25:23 And on the morrow when Agrippa was come and Bernice, with great pompe, and was entred into the place of hearing, with the chiefe captaines, and principall men of the citie; at Festus commaundement Paul was brought foorth.

text.NRSV: And Festus said, “King Agrippa and all here present with us, you see this man about whom the whole Jewish community petitioned me, both in Jerusalem and here, shouting that he ought not to live any longer.
text.Rotherham: And Festus saith—King Agrippa! and all ye men, here present with us: Ye observe this person, concerning whom, one and all the throng of the Jews, have interceded with me, both in Jerusalem and here, crying aloud that he ought not to be living any longer.
text.KJV: 25:24 And Festus said, King Agrippa, and all men which are heere present with vs, ye see this man, about whom all the multitude of the Iewes haue dealt with me, both at Hierusalem, and also heere, crying that he ought not to liue any longer.

text.NRSV: But I found that he had done nothing deserving death; and when he himself appealed to his Imperial Majesty, I decided to send him.
text.Rotherham: But, I, gathered, that, nothing worthy of death, had he committed; and, this man himself, having appealed unto the Emperor, I decided to send him:—
text.KJV: 25:25 But when I found that he had committed nothing worthy of death, and that he himselfe hath appealed to Augustus, I haue determined to send him.

text.NRSV: But I have nothing definite to write to our sovereign about him. Therefore I have brought him before all of you, and especially before you, King Agrippa, so that, after we have examined him, I may have something to write—
text.Rotherham: Concerning whom, anything certain to write unto my lord, I have not; wherefore, I have brought him forth before you,—and especially before thee, King Agrippa! in order that, after examination had, I might have something I could write;
text.KJV: 25:26 Of whom I haue no certaine thing to write vnto my Lord: Wherefore I haue brought him foorth before you, and specially before thee, O king Agrippa, that after examination had, I might haue somewhat to write.

text.NRSV: for it seems to me unreasonable to send a prisoner without indicating the charges against him.”
text.Rotherham: For, unreasonable, unto me, it seemeth, when sending a prisoner, not also, the accusations against him, to signify.
text.KJV: 25:27 For it seemeth to me vnreasonable, to send a prisoner, and not withall to signifie the crimes laid against him.

text.NRSV: Agrippa said to Paul, “You have permission to speak for yourself.” Then Paul stretched out his hand and began his defense:
text.Rotherham: And, Agrippa, to Paul, said—It is permitted thee, on thine own behalf, to be speaking. Then Paul, stretching forth his hand, went on to make his defence.
text.KJV: 26:1 Then Agrippa said vnto Paul, Thou art permitted to speake for thy selfe. Then Paul stretched foorth the hand, and answered for himselfe,

text.NRSV: “I consider myself fortunate that it is before you, King Agrippa, I am to make my defense today against all the accusations of the Jews,
text.Rotherham: Concerning all things of which I am accused by Jews, King Agrippa, I have been counting myself happy, that, before thee, am I about, this day, to be making my defence;
text.KJV: 26:2 I thinke my selfe happy, king Agrippa, because I shall answere for my selfe this day before thee touching all the things whereof I am accused of the Iewes:

text.NRSV: especially because you are familiar with all the customs and controversies of the Jews; therefore I beg you to listen to me patiently.
text.Rotherham: especially, as thou art, well-versed, in all the Jewish customs and questions. Wherefore, I beseech thee, patiently, to hear me.
text.KJV: 26:3 Especially, because I know thee to be expert in all customes and questions which are among the Iewes: wherefore I beseech thee to heare mee patiently.

text.NRSV: “All the Jews know my way of life from my youth, a life spent from the beginning among my own people and in Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: My manner of life, then, from my youth, which, from its commencement, was formed among my nation, even in Jerusalem, know all Jews,
text.KJV: 26:4 My maner of life from my youth, which was at the first among mine owne nation at Hierusalem, know all the Iewes,

text.NRSV: They have known for a long time, if they are willing to testify, that I have lived as a Pharisee according to the strictest sect of our religion.
text.Rotherham: inasmuch as they were aforetime observing me, from the outset,—if they please to bear witness,—that, according to the strictest sect of our own religion, I lived, a Pharisee.
text.KJV: 26:5 Which knew me from the beginning, (if they would testifie) that after the most straitest sect of our religion, I liued a Pharisee.

text.NRSV: And now I stand here on trial on account of my hope in the promise made by God to our ancestors,
text.Rotherham: And, now, for the hope of the promise, unto our fathers, being brought to pass by God, am I standing to be judged,—
text.KJV: 26:6 And now I stand, and am iudged for the hope of the promise made of God vnto our fathers:

text.NRSV: a promise that our twelve tribes hope to attain, as they earnestly worship day and night. It is for this hope, your Majesty, that I am accused by Jews!
text.Rotherham: unto which [hope], our twelve-tribed nation, with intensity, night and day, rendering divine service, is hoping to attain—concerning which hope, I am being accused by Jews, O King!
text.KJV: 26:7 Vnto which promise our twelue tribes instantly seruing God day and night, hope to come: For which hopes sake, King Agrippa, I am accused of the Iewes.

text.NRSV: Why is it thought incredible by any of you that God raises the dead?
text.Rotherham: What! incredible, is it judged with you, that, God the dead doth raise?
text.KJV: 26:8 Why should it be thought a thing incredible with you, that God should raise the dead?

text.NRSV: “Indeed, I myself was convinced that I ought to do many things against the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
text.Rotherham: I, therefore, imagined to myself, that, against the name of Jesus the Nazarene, it was needful, many hostile things, to bring about,—
text.KJV: 26:9 I verily thought with my selfe, that I ought to doe many things contrary to the name of Iesus of Nazareth:

text.NRSV: And that is what I did in Jerusalem; with authority received from the chief priests, I not only locked up many of the saints in prison, but I also cast my vote against them when they were condemned to death.
text.Rotherham: which also I did in Jerusalem, yea and, many of the saints, I myself, in prisons, shut up,—the authority, from the High-priests, having received: and, when they were to be put to death, I brought against them my vote;
text.KJV: 26:10 Which thing I also did in Hierusalem, and many of the Saints did I shut vp in prison, hauing receiued authoritie from the chiefe Priests, and when they were put to death, I gaue my voyce against them.

text.NRSV: By punishing them often in all the synagogues I tried to force them to blaspheme; and since I was so furiously enraged at them, I pursued them even to foreign cities.
text.Rotherham: and, throughout all the synagogues, ofttimes punishing them, I would fain have compelled them to defame; and, being excessively maddened against them, I went on to pursue them as far as even the outlying cities.
text.KJV: 26:11 And I punished them oft in euery Synagogue, and compelled them to blaspheme, and being exceedingly mad against them, I persecuted them euen vnto strange cities.

text.NRSV: “With this in mind I was traveling to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests,
text.Rotherham: Among which things, being on a journey unto Damascus, with the authority and commission of the High-priests,
text.KJV: 26:12 Whereupon, as I went to Damascus, with authoritie and commission from the chiefe Priests:

text.NRSV: when at midday along the road, your Majesty, I saw a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining around me and my companions.
text.Rotherham: at mid-day, on the road, I saw, O King, from heaven, above the splendour of the sun, shining around me, a light, and [around] them who, with me, were journeying;
text.KJV: 26:13 At midday, O king, I saw in the way a light from heauen, aboue the brightnes of the Sunne, shining round about mee, and them which iourneyed with me.

text.NRSV: When we had all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew language, ‘Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It hurts you to kick against the goads.’
text.Rotherham: and, when we were all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice, saying unto me, in the Hebrew language—Saul! Saul! why, me, art thou persecuting? It is hard for thee, against goads, to be kicking!
text.KJV: 26:14 And when wee were all fallen to the earth, I heard a voice speaking vnto me, and saying in the Hebrew tongue, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? It is hard for thee to kicke against the prickes.

text.NRSV: I asked, ‘Who are you, Lord?’ And the Lord answered, ‘I am Jesus whom you are persecuting.
text.Rotherham: And, I, said—Who art thou, Lord? And the Lord said—I, am Jesus, whom, thou, art persecuting!
text.KJV: 26:15 And I said, Who art thou, Lord? And hee said, I am Iesus whom thou persecutest.

text.NRSV: But get up and stand on your feet; for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you to serve and testify to the things in which you have seen me and to those in which I will appear to you.
text.Rotherham: But rise and stand upon thy feet; for, to this end, have I appeared unto thee,—To appoint thee an attendant and a witness, both of the things as to which thou hast seen me, and of those as to which I will appear unto thee:
text.KJV: 26:16 But rise, and stand vpon thy feete, for I haue appeared vnto thee for this purpose, to make thee a minister and a witnesse, both of these things which thou hast seene, & of those things in the which I will appeare vnto thee,

text.NRSV: I will rescue you from your people and from the Gentiles—to whom I am sending you
text.Rotherham: Rescuing thee from among the people, and from among the nations, unto whom, I, am sending thee—
text.KJV: 26:17 Deliuering thee from the people, and from the Gentiles, vnto whom now I send thee,

text.NRSV: to open their eyes so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, so that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me.’
text.Rotherham: To open their eyes; that they turn from darkness unto light, and the authority of Satan unto God, that they may receive remission of sins, and an inheritance among them who have been made holy by the faith respecting me.
text.KJV: 26:18 To open their eyes, and to turne them from darknesse to light, and from the power of Satan vnto God, that they may receiue forgiuenesse of sinnes, and inheritance among them which are sanctified by faith that is in me.

text.NRSV: “After that, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision,
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, O King Agrippa,—I became not disobedient unto the heavenly vision;
text.KJV: 26:19 Whereupon, O king Agrippa, I was not disobedient vnto the heauenly vision:

text.NRSV: but declared first to those in Damascus, then in Jerusalem and throughout the countryside of Judea, and also to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God and do deeds consistent with repentance.
text.Rotherham: But—both to them in Damascus, first, and in Jerusalem, unto all the country of Judaea also, and unto the nations, I carried tidings—that they should repent, and turn unto God, and, works worthy of their repentance, should practise.
text.KJV: 26:20 But shewed first vnto them of Damascus, and at Hierusalem, and thorowout all the coasts of Iudea, and then to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turne to God, and do works meete for repentance.

text.NRSV: For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me.
text.Rotherham: Because of these things, Jews seized me in the temple, and were attempting to slay me with their own hands.
text.KJV: 26:21 For these causes the Iewes caught mee in the Temple, and went about to kill me.

text.NRSV: To this day I have had help from God, and so I stand here, testifying to both small and great, saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would take place:
text.Rotherham: So then, having met with, the help that is from God, until this day, do I stand, witnessing to both small and great, nothing else saying, than those things which both the prophets, and Moses, did say should certainly come to pass:—
text.KJV: 26:22 Hauing therefore obteined helpe of God, I continue vnto this day, witnessing both to small and great, saying none other things then those which the Prophets and Moses did say should come:

text.NRSV: that the Messiah must suffer, and that, by being the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles.”
text.Rotherham: If, to suffer, the Christ was destined, if, the first of a resurrection of the dead, he is about to carry tidings, of light, both unto the people, and unto the nations.
text.KJV: 26:23 That Christ should suffer, and that hee should be the first that should rise from the dead, & should shew light vnto the people, and to the Gentiles.

text.NRSV: While he was making this defense, Festus exclaimed, “You are out of your mind, Paul! Too much learning is driving you insane!”
text.Rotherham: Now, as he was saying these things in his defence, Festus, with a loud voice, saith—Thou art raving, Paul! Thy great learning, is turning thee round unto, raving madness.
text.KJV: 26:24 And as hee thus spake for himselfe, Festus saide with a lowd voyce, Paul, thou art beside thy selfe, much learning doeth make thee mad.

text.NRSV: But Paul said, “I am not out of my mind, most excellent Festus, but I am speaking the sober truth.
text.Rotherham: But Paul—I am not raving (saith he), most noble Festus,—but, the declarations of truth and soberness, am I sounding forth:
text.KJV: 26:25 But he said, I am not mad, most noble Festus, but speake foorth the words of trueth and sobernesse.

text.NRSV: Indeed the king knows about these things, and to him I speak freely; for I am confident that none of these things has escaped his notice, for this was not done in a corner.
text.Rotherham: For well-knoweth, the king, concerning these things, unto whom, with boldness of utterance, am I speaking; for, that these things are not hidden from him, I am well persuaded,—for, not in a corner, hath this thing been done.
text.KJV: 26:26 For the King knoweth of these things, before whom also I speake freely: for I am perswaded, that none of these things are hidden from him, for this thing was not done in a corner.

text.NRSV: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe.”
text.Rotherham: Believest thou, King Agrippa, in the prophets? I know that thou believest!
text.KJV: 26:27 King Agrippa, beleeuest thou the Prophets? I know that thou beleeuest.

text.NRSV: Agrippa said to Paul, “Are you so quickly persuading me to become a Christian?”
text.Rotherham: And, Agrippa, [said] unto Paul—Almost, art thou persuading, me, to become a, Christian!
text.KJV: 26:28 Then Agrippa saide vnto Paul, Almost thou perswadest mee to bee a Christian.

text.NRSV: Paul replied, “Whether quickly or not, I pray to God that not only you but also all who are listening to me today might become such as I am—except for these chains.”
text.Rotherham: And, Paul, [answered] —I could pray unto God that, both almost and altogether, not only thou but all they who are hearing me this day, might become such,—as even, I, am, excepting these bonds.
text.KJV: 26:29 And Paul said, I would to God, that not onely thou, but also all that heare mee this day, were both almost, and altogether such as I am, except these bonds.

text.NRSV: Then the king got up, and with him the governor and Bernice and those who had been sitting with them;
text.Rotherham: And the king rose up, and the governor, Bernice also, and they who had been sitting with them;
text.KJV: 26:30 And when hee had thus spoken, the king rose vp, and the gouernour, and Bernice, & they that sate with them.

text.NRSV: and as they were leaving, they said to one another, “This man is doing nothing to deserve death or imprisonment.”
text.Rotherham: And, retiring, they began conversing one with another, saying, Nothing worthy of death or of bonds, doth this man practise.
text.KJV: 26:31 And when they were gone aside, they talked betweene themselues, saying, This man doeth nothing worthy of death, or of bonds.

text.NRSV: Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to the emperor.”
text.Rotherham: And, Agrippa, unto Festus, said—This man might have been released, if he had not appealed unto Caesar.
text.KJV: 26:32 Then said Agrippa vnto Festus, This man might haue bene set at libertie, if he had not appealed vnto Cesar.

text.NRSV: When it was decided that we were to sail for Italy, they transferred Paul and some other prisoners to a centurion of the Augustan Cohort, named Julius.
text.Rotherham: Now, when it was determined that we should sail for Italy, they proceeded to deliver Paul, and certain other prisoners, unto a centurion by name Julius, of an Augustan band.
text.KJV: 27:1 And when it was determined, that wee should saile into Italy, they deliuered Paul, & certaine other prisoners, vnto one named Iulius, a centurion of Augustus band.

text.NRSV: Embarking on a ship of Adramyttium that was about to set sail to the ports along the coast of Asia, we put to sea, accompanied by Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica.
text.Rotherham: And, going on board a ship of Adramittium, about to sail unto the places along the coast of Asia, we put to sea, there being with us, Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica;
text.KJV: 27:2 And entring into a ship of Adramyttium, wee lanched, meaning to saile by the coasts of Asia, one Aristarchus a Macedonian, of Thessalonica, beeing with vs.

text.NRSV: The next day we put in at Sidon; and Julius treated Paul kindly, and allowed him to go to his friends to be cared for.
text.Rotherham: and, on the next day, we put into Zidon,—and Julius, treating Paul, kindly, gave him leave to go unto his friends and refresh himself;
text.KJV: 27:3 And the next day wee touched at Sidon: And Iulius courteously entreated Paul, and gaue him libertie to goe vnto his friends to refresh himselfe.

text.NRSV: Putting out to sea from there, we sailed under the lee of Cyprus, because the winds were against us.
text.Rotherham: and, from thence putting out to sea, we sailed under the lee of Cyprus, because the winds were contrary;
text.KJV: 27:4 And when we had lanched from thence, we sailed vnder Cyprus, because the winds were contrary.

text.NRSV: After we had sailed across the sea that is off Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came to Myra in Lycia.
text.Rotherham: and, sailing across the sea which is off Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came down to Myra, a city of Lycia.
text.KJV: 27:5 And when we had sailed ouer the sea of Cilicia and Pamphylia, wee came to Myra a citie of Lysia.

text.NRSV: There the centurion found an Alexandrian ship bound for Italy and put us on board.
text.Rotherham: And the centurion, there, finding a ship of Alexandria sailing for Italy, put us therein.
text.KJV: 27:6 And there the Centurion found a ship of Alexandria sailing into Italy, and he put vs therein.

text.NRSV: We sailed slowly for a number of days and arrived with difficulty off Cnidus, and as the wind was against us, we sailed under the lee of Crete off Salmone.
text.Rotherham: And, for a good many days sailing slowly, and getting with difficulty over against Cnidus, the wind not suffering us to get on, we sailed under the lee of Crete, over against Salmone;
text.KJV: 27:7 And when wee had sailed slowly many dayes, and scarse were come ouer against Gnidus, the wind not suffering vs, wee sailed vnder Creete, ouer against Salmone,

text.NRSV: Sailing past it with difficulty, we came to a place called Fair Havens, near the city of Lasea.
text.Rotherham: and, with difficulty coasting it, we came to a certain place called Fair Havens, near to which was the city of Lasea.
text.KJV: 27:8 And hardly passing it, came vnto a place which is called the Faire hauens, nigh whereunto was the citie of Lasea.

text.NRSV: Since much time had been lost and sailing was now dangerous, because even the Fast had already gone by, Paul advised them,
text.Rotherham: And, when a considerable time had passed, and sailing was already dangerous, because, even the Fast, had already gone by, Paul began to advise,
text.KJV: 27:9 Now when much time was spent, and when sailing was now dangerous, because the Fast was now alreadie past, Paul admonished them,

text.NRSV: saying, “Men, I can see that the voyage will be with danger and heavy loss, not only of the cargo and the ship, but also of our lives.”
text.Rotherham: saying to them—Sirs! I perceive that, with damage and great loss, not only of the cargo and of the ship, but even of our persons, shall the voyage certainly be attended.
text.KJV: 27:10 And said vnto them, Sirs, I perceiue that this voyage will be with hurt and much damage, not onely of the lading & ship, but also of our liues.

text.NRSV: But the centurion paid more attention to the pilot and to the owner of the ship than to what Paul said.
text.Rotherham: But, the centurion, by the master, and by the shipowner, was more persuaded than by the things which, by Paul, were spoken.
text.KJV: 27:11 Neuerthelesse, the Centurion beleeued the master and the owner of the shippe, more then those things which were spoken by Paul.

text.NRSV: Since the harbor was not suitable for spending the winter, the majority was in favor of putting to sea from there, on the chance that somehow they could reach Phoenix, a harbor of Crete, facing southwest and northwest, and spend the winter there.
text.Rotherham: And, the harbour being, incommodious, to winter in, the more part, advised to put to sea from thence, if by any means they might be able to reach Phoenix, to winter, [which was] a harbour of Crete, looking north-east and south-east.
text.KJV: 27:12 And because the hauen was not commodious to winter in, the more part aduised to depart thence also, if by any meanes they might attaine to Phenice, and there to winter; which is an hauen of Creete, and lieth toward the Southwest, and Northwest.

text.NRSV: When a moderate south wind began to blow, they thought they could achieve their purpose; so they weighed anchor and began to sail past Crete, close to the shore.
text.Rotherham: And, a south wind blowing softly, supposing they had secured their purpose, weighing anchor, they began to sail close in shore along Crete.
text.KJV: 27:13 And when the South wind blew softly, supposing that they had obtained their purpose, loosing thence, they sailed close by Creete.

text.NRSV: But soon a violent wind, called the Northeaster, rushed down from the island.
text.Rotherham: But, after no long time, there beat down from it a tempestuous wind, called Euraquilo,—
text.KJV: 27:14 But not long after, there arose against it a tempestuous winde, called Euroclydon.

text.NRSV: Since the ship was caught and could not be turned head to the wind, we gave way to it and were driven.
text.Rotherham: and, the ship being caught and we not being able to bring her head to the wind, we let her go, and were borne along.
text.KJV: 27:15 And when the ship was caught, and could not beare vp into the winde, we let her driue.

text.NRSV: By running under the lee of a small island called Cauda, we were scarcely able to get the ship’s boat under control.
text.Rotherham: And, running under the lee, of some small island, called Cauda, we were able, with difficulty, to make ourselves masters of the boat,—
text.KJV: 27:16 And running vnder a certaine yland, which is called Clauda, wee had much worke to come by the boate:

text.NRSV: After hoisting it up they took measures to undergird the ship; then, fearing that they would run on the Syrtis, they lowered the sea anchor and so were driven.
text.Rotherham: which, hoisting up, they began to use, helps, under-girding the ship; and, fearing lest, on the sand-bank of Africa, they should run aground, lowering the gear, so, were they borne along.
text.KJV: 27:17 Which when they had taken vp, they vsed helps, vnder-girding the ship; and fearing lest they should fall into the quicke-sands, strake saile, and so were driuen.

text.NRSV: We were being pounded by the storm so violently that the next day they began to throw the cargo overboard,
text.Rotherham: But, we being exceedingly tempest-tossed, on the next day, they began to throw [cargo] overboard;
text.KJV: 27:18 And being exceedingly tossed with a tempest the next day, they lightened the ship:

text.NRSV: and on the third day with their own hands they threw the ship’s tackle overboard.
text.Rotherham: and, on the third day, with their own hands, the tackling of the ship, they cast away.
text.KJV: 27:19 And the third day we cast out with our owne handes the tackling of the shippe.

text.NRSV: When neither sun nor stars appeared for many days, and no small tempest raged, all hope of our being saved was at last abandoned.
text.Rotherham: And, neither, sun nor stars, appearing for many days, and, no small tempest, lying upon us, in the end, all hope that we should be saved, began to be taken from us.
text.KJV: 27:20 And when neither Sunne nor starres in many dayes appeared, and no small tempest lay on vs; all hope that wee should be saued, was then taken away.

text.NRSV: Since they had been without food for a long time, Paul then stood up among them and said, “Men, you should have listened to me and not have set sail from Crete and thereby avoided this damage and loss.
text.Rotherham: But, when they had been, long without food, then, Paul, standing in the midst of them, said—Ye ought, indeed, Sir! yielding to me, not to have sailed away from Crete, to get this damage and loss. …
text.KJV: 27:21 But after long abstinence, Paul stood foorth in the middes of them, and said, Sirs, yee should haue hearkened vnto mee, and not haue loosed from Creete, and to haue gained this harme and losse.

text.NRSV: I urge you now to keep up your courage, for there will be no loss of life among you, but only of the ship.
text.Rotherham: And, now, I recommend you to be of good courage; for, loss of life, shall there be, none at all, from among you,—only the ship.
text.KJV: 27:22 And now I exhort you to be of good cheere: for there shall be no losse of any mans life among you, but of the shippe.

text.NRSV: For last night there stood by me an angel of the God to whom I belong and whom I worship,
text.Rotherham: For there stood by me this night, belonging unto the God whose I am, unto whom also I am doing divine service, a messenger,
text.KJV: 27:23 For there stood by me this night the Angel of God, whose I am, and whom I serue,

text.NRSV: and he said, ‘Do not be afraid, Paul; you must stand before the emperor; and indeed, God has granted safety to all those who are sailing with you.’
text.Rotherham: saying—Be not afraid, Paul! for, before Caesar, must thou needs stand. And lo! God hath granted to thee as a favour, all them who are sailing with thee.
text.KJV: 27:24 Saying, Feare not Paul, thou must be brought before Cesar, and loe, God hath giuen thee all them that saile with thee.

text.NRSV: So keep up your courage, men, for I have faith in God that it will be exactly as I have been told.
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, be of good courage, Sirs; for I believe in God—that, so, it shall be, according as it hath been told me.
text.KJV: 27:25 Wherefore, sirs, be of good cheere: for I beleeue God, that it shall be euen as it was tolde me.

text.NRSV: But we will have to run aground on some island.”
text.Rotherham: Upon a certain island, however, must we needs be wrecked.
text.KJV: 27:26 Howbeit, we must be cast vpon a certaine Iland.

text.NRSV: When the fourteenth night had come, as we were drifting across the sea of Adria, about midnight the sailors suspected that they were nearing land.
text.Rotherham: And, when, the fourteenth night, had come, and we were being driven to and fro in the Adriatic, about midnight, the sailors suspected that some country was, nearing, them;
text.KJV: 27:27 But when the fourteenth night was come, as wee were driuen vp and downe in Adria about midnight, the shipmen deemed that they drew neere to some countrey:

text.NRSV: So they took soundings and found twenty fathoms; a little farther on they took soundings again and found fifteen fathoms.
text.Rotherham: and, sounding, they found twenty fathoms,—and, going a little further, and again sounding, they found fifteen fathoms.
text.KJV: 27:28 And sounded, and found it twentie fathoms: and when they had gone a little further, they sounded againe, and found it fifteene fathoms.

text.NRSV: Fearing that we might run on the rocks, they let down four anchors from the stern and prayed for day to come.
text.Rotherham: And, fearing lest haply, on rocky places, we should be wrecked, out of stern, cast they four anchors,—and began praying that day might dawn.
text.KJV: 27:29 Then fearing lest we should haue fallen vpon rockes, they cast foure ancres out of the sterne, and wished for the day.

text.NRSV: But when the sailors tried to escape from the ship and had lowered the boat into the sea, on the pretext of putting out anchors from the bow,
text.Rotherham: But, when, the sailors, were seeking to flee out of the ship, and had lowered the boat into the sea, by pretext, as though out of the prow they had been about to reach anchors,
text.KJV: 27:30 And as the shipmen were about to flee out of the ship, when they had let downe the boat into the sea, vnder colour as though they would haue cast ancres out of the fore-ship,

text.NRSV: Paul said to the centurion and the soldiers, “Unless these men stay in the ship, you cannot be saved.”
text.Rotherham: Paul said unto the centurion, and unto the soldiers—Except, these, abide in the ship, ye yourselves, cannot be saved!
text.KJV: 27:31 Paul said to the Centurion, and to the souldiers, Except these abide in the ship, ye cannot be saued.

text.NRSV: Then the soldiers cut away the ropes of the boat and set it adrift.
text.Rotherham: Then, the soldiers cut away the ropes of the boat, and let her fall off.
text.KJV: 27:32 Then the souldiers cut off the ropes of the boat, and let her fall off.

text.NRSV: Just before daybreak, Paul urged all of them to take some food, saying, “Today is the fourteenth day that you have been in suspense and remaining without food, having eaten nothing.
text.Rotherham: And, until day was about to dawn, Paul continued to beseech one and all to take some food, saying—This day is, the fourteenth day, that, suspense, fasting, ye are completing,—having helped yourselves, to nothing.
text.KJV: 27:33 And while the day was comming on, Paul besought them all to take meat, saying, This day is the fourteenth day that ye haue taried, and continued fasting, hauing taken nothing.

text.NRSV: Therefore I urge you to take some food, for it will help you survive; for none of you will lose a hair from your heads.”
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, I beseech you to take some food,—for, this, lays a foundation for your safety; for, of no one of you, shall a hair of the head perish.
text.KJV: 27:34 Wherefore, I pray you to take some meat, for this is for your health: for there shall not an haire fall from the head of any of you.

text.NRSV: After he had said this, he took bread; and giving thanks to God in the presence of all, he broke it and began to eat.
text.Rotherham: And, having said these things, and taken a loaf, he gave thanks unto God before all, and, breaking it, began to eat.
text.KJV: 27:35 And when hee had thus spoken, hee tooke bread, and gaue thankes to God in presence of them all, and when he had broken it, he began to eate.

text.NRSV: Then all of them were encouraged and took food for themselves.
text.Rotherham: And, all becoming, of good cheer, they also, helped themselves to food.
text.KJV: 27:36 Then were they all of good cheere, and they also tooke some meat.

text.NRSV: (We were in all two hundred seventy-six persons on the ship.)
text.Rotherham: Now we were, in the ship, in all, about seventy-six souls.
text.KJV: 27:37 And we were in all, in the ship, two hundred, threescore and sixteene soules.

text.NRSV: After they had satisfied their hunger, they lightened the ship by throwing the wheat into the sea.
text.Rotherham: And, when they were satisfied with food, they began lightening the ship, casting out the wheat into the sea.
text.KJV: 27:38 And when they had eaten enough, they lightened the ship, and cast out the wheat into the sea.

text.NRSV: In the morning they did not recognize the land, but they noticed a bay with a beach, on which they planned to run the ship ashore, if they could.
text.Rotherham: And, when day came, they could not recognise, the land; but perceived, a certain bay, having a beach,—upon which they were minded, if they could, safely to bring the ship.
text.KJV: 27:39 And when it was day, they knew not the land: but they discouered a certaine creek, with a shore, into the which they were minded, if it were possible, to thrust in the ship.

text.NRSV: So they cast off the anchors and left them in the sea. At the same time they loosened the ropes that tied the steering-oars; then hoisting the foresail to the wind, they made for the beach.
text.Rotherham: And, casting off the anchors, they let them go into the sea,—at the same time, loosening the lashings of the rudders, and, hoisting up the foresail to the wind, they made for the beach.
text.KJV: 27:40 And when they had taken vp the ankers, they committed themselues vnto the sea, & loosed the rudder bands, and hoised vp the maine saile to the winde, and made toward shore.

text.NRSV: But striking a reef, they ran the ship aground; the bow stuck and remained immovable, but the stern was being broken up by the force of the waves.
text.Rotherham: But, falling into a place where two seas met, they ran the ship aground; and, the foreship sticking fast, remained immoveable, while, the stern, began to break up, from the violence [of the waves] .
text.KJV: 27:41 And falling into a place where two seas met, they ranne the shippe a ground, and the forepart stucke fast, and remained vnmoueable, but the hinder part was broken with the violence of the waues.

text.NRSV: The soldiers’ plan was to kill the prisoners, so that none might swim away and escape;
text.Rotherham: Now, the soldiers counsel, turned out to be, that they should kill, the prisoners, lest any one should swim out and escape;
text.KJV: 27:42 And the souldiers counsel was to kil the prisoners, lest any of them should swimme out, and escape.

text.NRSV: but the centurion, wishing to save Paul, kept them from carrying out their plan. He ordered those who could swim to jump overboard first and make for the land,
text.Rotherham: but, the centurion, being minded to bring Paul safely through, hindered them of their purpose, and ordered such as were able to swim, to cast themselves overboard and, get first to the land,—
text.KJV: 27:43 But the Centurion, willing to saue Paul, kept them from their purpose, and commanded that they which could swimme, should cast themselues first into the sea, and get to land:

text.NRSV: and the rest on planks or on pieces of the ship. And so it was that all were brought safely to land.
text.Rotherham: and, the rest, some, on planks, and, some, on other things from the ship, …and, so, it came to pass, that, all, were brought safely through, on to the land.
text.KJV: 27:44 And the rest, some on boords, and some on broken pieces of the ship: and so it came to passe that they escaped all safe to land.

text.Base: After we had reached safety, we then learned that the island was called Malta.
text.KJV: 28:1 And when they were escaped, then they knew that the Iland was called Melita.

text.Base: The natives showed us unusual kindness. Since it had begun to rain and was cold, they kindled a fire and welcomed all of us around it.
text.KJV: 28:2 And the barbarous people shewed vs no little kindnesse: for they kindled a fire, and receiued vs euery one because of the present raine, and because of the cold.

text.Base: Paul had gathered a bundle of brushwood and was putting it on the fire, when a viper, driven out by the heat, fastened itself on his hand.
text.KJV: 28:3 And when Paul had gathered a bundle of stickes, and layde them on the fire, there came a Uiper out of the heat, and fastened on his hand.

text.Base: When the natives saw the creature hanging from his hand, they said to one another, “This man must be a murderer; though he has escaped from the sea, justice has not allowed him to live.”
text.KJV: 28:4 And when the Barbarians saw the venomous beast hang on his hand, they saide among themselues, No doubt this man is a murtherer, whom though hee hath escaped the Sea, yet Uengeance suffereth not to liue.

text.Base: But he shook off the creature into the fire and suffered no harm.
text.KJV: 28:5 And hee shooke off the beast into the fire, and felt no harme.

text.Base: They were expecting him to swell up or drop dead, but after they had waited a long time and saw that nothing unusual happened to him, they changed their minds and began to say that he was a god.
text.KJV: 28:6 Howbeit, they looked when hee should haue swollen, or fallen downe dead suddenly: but after they had looked a great while, and saw no harme come to him, they changed their minds, and said that he was a God.

text.Base: Now in the neighborhood of that place were lands belonging to the leading man of the island, named Publius, who received us and entertained us hospitably for three days.
text.KJV: 28:7 In the same quarters were possessions of the chiefe man of the Iland, whose name was Publius, who receiued vs, and lodged vs three dayes courteously.

text.Base: It so happened that the father of Publius lay sick in bed with fever and dysentery. Paul visited him and cured him by praying and laying his hands on him.
text.KJV: 28:8 And it came to passe that the father of Publius lay sicke of a feuer, and of a bloody-flixe, to whom Paul entred in, and prayed, and layed his hands on him, and healed him.

text.Base: After this happened, the rest of the people on the island who had diseases also came and were cured.
text.KJV: 28:9 So when this was done, others also which had diseases in the Iland, came, and were healed:

text.Base: They bestowed many honors on us, and when we were about to sail, they put on board all the provisions we needed.
text.KJV: 28:10 Who also honoured vs with many honours, and when wee departed, they laded vs with such things as were necessary.

text.Base: Three months later we set sail on an Alexandrian ship that had wintered at the island, with the Twin Brothers as its figurehead.
text.KJV: 28:11 And after three moneths wee departed in a ship of Alexandria, which had wintered in the Ile, whose signe was Castor and Pollux.

text.Base: We put in at Syracuse and stayed there for three days;
text.KJV: 28:12 And landing at Syracuse wee taried there three dayes.

text.Base: then we weighed anchor and came to Rhegium. After one day a south wind sprang up, and on the second day we came to Puteoli.
text.KJV: 28:13 And from thence wee fet a compasse, and came to Rhegium, and after one day the South winde blew, and we came the next day to Puteoli:

text.Base: There we found believers and were invited to stay with them for seven days. And so we came to Rome.
text.KJV: 28:14 Where wee found brethren, and were desired to tary with them seuen dayes: and so we went toward Rome.

text.Base: The believers from there, when they heard of us, came as far as the Forum of Appius and Three Taverns to meet us. On seeing them, Paul thanked God and took courage.
text.KJV: 28:15 And from thence, when the brethren heard of vs, they came to meet vs as farre as Appii forum, and the three Tauernes: whom when Paul saw, he thanked God, and tooke courage.

text.Base: When we came into Rome, Paul was allowed to live by himself, with the soldier who was guarding him.
text.KJV: 28:16 And when we came to Rome, the Centurion deliuered the prisoners to the Captaine of the guard: but Paul was suffered to dwell by himselfe, with a souldier that kept him.

text.Base: Three days later he called together the local leaders of the Jews. When they had assembled, he said to them, “Brothers, though I had done nothing against our people or the customs of our ancestors, yet I was arrested in Jerusalem and handed over to the Romans.
text.KJV: 28:17 And it came to passe, that after three dayes, Paul called the chiefe of the Iewes together. And when they were come together, he said vnto them, Men and brethren, though I haue committed nothing against the people, or customes of our fathers, yet was I deliuered prisoner from Hierusalem into the hands of the Romanes.

text.Base: When they had examined me, they wanted to release me, because there was no reason for the death penalty in my case.
text.KJV: 28:18 Who when they had examined me, would haue let me goe, because there was no cause of death in me.

text.Base: But when the Jews objected, I was compelled to appeal to the emperor—even though I had no charge to bring against my nation.
text.KJV: 28:19 But when the Iewes spake against it, I was constrained to appeale vnto Cesar, not that I had ought to accuse my nation of.

text.Base: For this reason therefore I have asked to see you and speak with you, since it is for the sake of the hope of Israel that I am bound with this chain.”
text.KJV: 28:20 For this cause therefore haue I called for you, to see you, and to speake with you: because that for the hope of Israel I am bound with this chaine.

text.Base: They replied, “We have received no letters from Judea about you, and none of the brothers coming here has reported or spoken anything evil about you.
text.KJV: 28:21 And they saide vnto him, Wee neither receiued letters out of Iudea concerning thee, neither any of the brethren that came, shewed or spake any harme of thee.

text.Base: But we would like to hear from you what you think, for with regard to this sect we know that everywhere it is spoken against.”
text.KJV: 28:22 But we desire to heare of thee what thou thinkest: for as concerning this sect, we know that euery where it is spoken against.

text.Base: After they had set a day to meet with him, they came to him at his lodgings in still greater numbers. From morning until evening he explained the matter to them, testifying to the kingdom of God and trying to convince them about Jesus both from the law of Moses and from the prophets.
text.KJV: 28:23 And when they had appointed him a day, there came many to him into his lodging, to whom he expounded and testified the kingdome of God, perswading them concerning Iesus, both out of the law of Moses, and out of the Prophets, from morning till euening.

text.Base: Some were convinced by what he said, while others refused to believe.
text.KJV: 28:24 And some beleeued the things which were spoken, and some beleeued not.

text.Base: So they disagreed with each other; and as they were leaving, Paul made one further statement: “The Holy Spirit was right in saying to your ancestors through the prophet Isaiah,
text.KJV: 28:25 And when they agreed not among themselues, they departed, after that Paul had spoken one word, Well spake the holy Ghost by Esaias the Prophet, vnto our fathers,

text.Base: ‘Go to this people and say: You will indeed listen, but never understand, and you will indeed look, but never perceive.
text.KJV: 28:26 Saying, Goe vnto this people, and say, Hearing ye shall heare, and shall not vnderstand, and seeing ye shall see, and not perceiue.

text.Base: For this people’s heart has grown dull, and their ears are hard of hearing, and they have shut their eyes; so that they might not look with their eyes, and listen with their ears, and understand with their heart and turn— and I would heal them.’
text.KJV: 28:27 For the heart of this people is waxed grosse, and their eares are dull of hearing, and their eyes haue they closed, lest they should with their eyes, and heare with their eares, and vnderstand with their heart, and should bee conuerted, and I should heale them.

text.Base: “Let it be known to you then that this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles; they will listen.”
text.KJV: 28:28 Be it knowen therfore vnto you, that the saluation of God is sent vnto the Gentiles, and that they wil heare it.

text.Base: He lived there two whole years at his own expense and welcomed all who came to him,
text.KJV: 28:30 And Paul dwelt two whole yeeres in his owne hired house, and receiued all that came in vnto him,

text.Base: proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness and without hindrance.
text.KJV: 28:31 Preaching the kingdome of God, and teaching those things which concerne the Lord Iesus Christ, with all confidence, no man forbidding him.

8.1: And, Saul, was taking pleasure with them in his death. Moreover there arose, in that day, a great persecution against the assembly which was in Jerusalem; [and], all, were scattered abroad throughout the countries of Judaea and Samaria, except the apostles.
8.2: Howbeit reverent men assisted at the burial of Stephen, and made great lamentation over him.
8.3: But, Saul, went on to lay waste the assembly, along the houses, going in, and, dragging off both men and women, was
8.4: They, indeed, therefore, who were scattered abroad, passed through, telling the good-news of the word;
8.5: And, Philip, going down unto the city of Samaria, proclaimed unto them the Christ.
8.6: And the multitudes began to give heed unto the things that were being spoken by Philip, with one accord, when they heard him, and saw the signs which he was working.
8.7: For, from many who had impure spirits, shouting with a loud voice, they were going out, and, many that were paralysed and lame, were cured.
8.8: And there came to be great joy in that city.
8.9: But, a certain man named Simon, was already in the city, practising magical arts, and astonishing the nation of Samaria,—saying that himself was someone great:
8.10: Unto whom all were giving heed, from small even to great, saying—This, is the Power of God, which is called Great.
8.11: And they were giving heed to him, because that, for a considerable time, with his magical arts, he had astonished them.
8.12: But, when they believed Philip telling the good news concerning the kingdom of God, and the name of Jesus Christ, they proceeded to be immersed, both men and women.
8.13: And, Simon himself also, believed; and, having been immersed, was in constant attendance on Philip,—and, beholding signs and great works of power coming to pass, was astonished.
8.14: And the Apostles who were in Jerusalem, hearing that Samaria had welcomed the word of God, sent out unto them Peter and John;
8.15: Who, indeed, going down, prayed for them, that they might receive Holy Spirit;—
8.16: For, not yet, had it, upon any one of them, fallen, but, only, to begin with, they had been immersed into the name of the Lord Jesus.
8.17: Then, proceeded they to lay their hands upon them, and they were receiving Holy Spirit.
8.18: But Simon, seeing that, through means of the laying on of the hands of the Apostles, the Spirit was being given, offered unto them money,
8.19: Saying—Give unto me also this authority,—in order that, on whomsoever may lay my hands, he may receive Holy Spirit.
8.20: And Peter said unto him—Thy silver, with thee, go to destruction! Because, the free-gift of God, thou didst suppose could, with money, be obtained!
8.21: Thou hast neither part nor lot in this matter; for, thy heart, is not upright before God.
8.22: Repent, therefore, from this thy baseness, and entreat of the Lord—whether, after all, the purpose of thy heart shall be forgiven thee;
8.23: For see that thou servest as a gall-root of bitterness, and a bond of unrighteousness.
8.24: And Simon, answering, said—Entreat ye, in my behalf, unto the Lord; that, nothing, may come upon me, of the things whereof ye have spoken!
8.25: They, therefore, having fully borne witness, and spoken the word of the Lord, began their return unto Jerusalem, and, unto many villages of the Samaritans, were they telling the glad tidings.
8.26: And, a messenger of the Lord, spake unto Philip, saying—Arise, and be journeying along southward, unto the way that goeth down from Jerusalem unto Gaza,—the same, is desert.
8.27: And, arising, he journeyed. And lo! a man of Ethiopia, a eunuch, one in power under Candace queen of the Ethiopians, who was over all her treasure [who] had come to worship in Jerusalem;
8.28: And was returning, and, sitting in his chariot, and was reading the prophet Isaiah.
8.29: And the Spirit said unto Philip—Go near, and join thyself unto this chariot!
8.30: And, running near, Philip heard him reading Isaiah the prophet, and said—Dost thou, then, understand what thou art reading?
8.31: And, he, said—How indeed should be able—unless someone shall guide me? And he called upon Philip, to come up and sit with him.
8.32: Now, the passage of Scripture which he was reading, was, this:—As a sheep, unto slaughter, was he led, and, as a lamb, before him that sheareth it, is dumb, so, he openeth not his mouth.
8.33: In his humiliation, his judgment was taken away,—his generation, who shall describe? because, his life, is taken away from the earth.
8.34: And the eunuch, making answer unto Philip, said—I pray thee! Of whom, is the prophet saying this? of himself, or, of some different person?
8.35: And Philip, opening his mouth, and beginning from this scripture, told him the glad tidings of Jesus.
8.36: And, as they were journeying along the way, they came unto a certain water,—and the eunuch saith—Lo! water! What doth hinder my being immersed?
8.38: And he commanded the chariot to stand still; and they went down, both, into the water, both Philip and the eunuch,—and he immersed him.
8.39: But, when they came up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord caught away Philip, and the eunuch saw him no more; for he was going on his way rejoicing.
8.40: Now Philip was found at Azotus; and, passing through, he was telling the glad tidings unto all the cities, until he came unto Caesarea.

15.1: And, certain persons, coming down from Judea, began to teach the brethren—Except ye be circumcised according to the custom of Moses, ye cannot be saved.
15.2: And, when Paul and Barnabas had had no little dissension and discussion with them, it was arranged that Paul and Barnabas and certain others from among them should go up unto the Apostles and Elders in Jerusalem concerning this question,
15.3: They, therefore, being set forward by the Assembly, began passing through Phoenicia and Samaria, fully relating the conversion of them of the nations, and were causing great joy unto all the brethren.
15.4: And, having arrived in Jerusalem, they were welcomed by the Assembly and the Apostles and the Elders; and they recounted all things God had done with them.
15.5: But there [had] stood forth some of those who, from the sect of Pharisees, had believed, saying—It is needful to be circumcising them, also to charge them to be keeping the law of Moses.
15.6: And the Apostles and Elders were gathered together to see about this matter.
15.7: And, when much discussion had arisen, Peter standing up, said unto them—Brethren! Ye yourselves, well know that, in days long past, amongst you, God chose that, through my mouth, the nations should hear the word of the glad tidings, and believe.
15.8: And, the heart-observing God, bare witness,—unto them, giving the Holy Spirit, just as, even unto us;
15.9: and made no distinction at all betwixt us and them, by their faith, purifying their hearts.
15.10: Now, therefore, why are ye proving God, that ye should put a yoke upon the neck of the disciples, which, neither our fathers, nor we, have been able to bear.
15.11: But, through the favour of the Lord Jesus, we believe we shall be saved, in like manner as, even they.
15.12: And all the throng held their peace, and began to hearken unto Barnabas and Paul relating how many signs and wonders God had done among the nations, through them.
15.13: And, after they held their peace, James answered, saying—Brethren! hearken unto me.
15.14: Symeon, hath fully told how God, first visited, to take out of the nations, a people for his name.
15.15: And, with this, agree the words of the prophets, according as it is written—
15.16: After these things, will I return, and will rebuild the tent of David that hath fallen, and, the ruins thereof, will I rebuild, and will set it up again:
15.17: That the residues of men may seek out the Lord, and all the nations upon whom my name hath been called, saith the Lord that doeth these things,
15.19: Wherefore, I, judge, not to be troubling them who, from the nations, are turning unto God;
15.20: but to write unto them, to abstain from the pollutions of idols, and from fornication, and from what is strangled, and from blood.
15.21: For, Moses, out of ancient generation, hath, in every city, them who proclaim him; seeing, that in the synagogues, every sabbath, he is read.
15.22: Then, seemed it good unto the Apostles and the Elders with the whole Assembly to send chosen men from among them unto Antioch, with Paul and Barnabas,—even Judas who is called Barsabbas, and Silas, men taking a lead among the brethren:
15.23: writing through their hand—The Apostles and the Elder Brethren, unto the brethren throughout Antioch and Syria and Cilicia, who are from among the nations, wish joy!
15.24: Inasmuch as we had heard that, certain from among us, had troubled you with words, dismantling your souls,—unto whom we had given no instructions,
15.25: It seemed good unto us, coming to be of one accord, that we should choose men and send them unto you, with our beloved Barnabas and Paul,—
15.26: men who have given up their lives in behalf of the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.
15.27: We have sent, therefore, Judas and Silas, who also, themselves, by word of mouth, can tell you the same things.
15.28: For it hath seemed good unto the Holy Spirit and unto us, no greater burden, to be laying upon you, than these necessary things:—
15.29: To be abstaining from idol sacrifices, and from blood, and from what is strangled, and from fornication,—From which, if ye keep yourselves, ye shall prosper. Fare ye well.
15.30: They, therefore, being let go, came down unto Antioch; and, having gathered together the throng, delivered the letter,
15.31: and, when they read it, they rejoiced for the consolation.
15.32: And, both Judas and Silas, being themselves also prophets, with much discourse, consoled and confirmed the brethren.
15.33: And, when they had spent a time, they were let go, in peace, from the brethren, unto them who had sent them.
15.35: But, Paul and Barnabas, tarried in Antioch, teaching and telling the joyful tidings,—along with many others also,—of the word of the Lord.
15.36: And, after certain days, Paul, said unto Barnabas—Let us now return, and visit the brethren in every city in which we have declared the word of the Lord, and see how they are.
15.37: And, Barnabas, was minded to take with them John also, called Mark;
15.38: but Paul deemed it right, as to him who had withdrawn from them, back from Pamphylia, and had not gone with them unto the work, not to be taking with them, this, man.
15.39: And there arose an angry feeling, so that they separated one from the other: and, Barnabas, taking Mark, sailed away unto Cyprus,—
15.40: whereas, Paul, choosing Silas, went forth, committed unto the favour of the Lord by the brethren,
15.41: and proceeded to pass through Syria and Cilicia, confirming the assemblies.

23.1: And Paul, looking steadfastly, at the High-council, said—Brethren! I, in all good conscience have used my citizenship for God until this day.
23.2: And, the High-priest, Ananias, ordered them that stood by him, to be smiting him on the mouth.
23.3: Then, Paul, unto him, said—God is about to be smiting thee, thou whited wall! Dost, thou, then sit to judge me according to the law, and, unlawfully, orderest me to be smitten?
23.4: And, they who stood by, said—The High-priest of God, dost thou revile?
23.5: And Paul said—I was not aware, brethren, that he was high-priest; because it is written—Of a ruler of thy people, shalt thou not speak injuriously.
23.6: But Paul, getting to know that, the one part, were Sadducees and, the other, Pharisees, began to cry aloud in the council—Brethren! I, am, a Pharisee, son of Pharisees:—Concerning a hope, even of a rising again of the dead, am I to be judged.
23.7: And, as this he was saying, there arose a dissension of the Pharisees and Sadducees; and rent asunder was the throng.
23.8: For, Sadducees, say, there is no rising again, nor messenger, nor spirit, whereas, Pharisees, confess them both.
23.9: And there arose a great outcry, and certain of the Scribes of the party of the Pharisees, standing up, began to strive, saying—Nothing bad, find we in this man;—but, if a spirit hath spoken unto him, or a messenger…
23.10: And, great dissension arising, the captain, fearing lest Paul would be torn in pieces by them, ordered the troop to go down, and take him by force out of their midst, to bring him into the castle.
